topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the office was the loudest thing I had ever heard. Dust motes danced in the slivers of afternoon sun slicing through the blinds, illuminating the empty chair where Mr. Henderson used to sit. I kept tracing the outline of his worn leather desk with my fingertip, a nervous habit born of an inability to process the abrupt shift. It had been three weeks since the official announcement—a terse email stating his retirement. No farewell, no coffee invitation, just a void where decades of patient guidance used to reside. I stood by the window, watching the city rush by, the motion feeling alien and relentless. My shoulders ached, a dull, persistent throb that mirrored the heavy weight settling in my chest. Every time I tried to focus on the pending project, my gaze drifted back to the closed door, a palpable sense of unfinished business hanging in the air.

[story 2]

I walked into the study expecting the familiar, comforting presence of Professor Albright, but the room felt strangely sterile. The framed photographs on the mantelpiece seemed to mock my current state—snapshots of shared triumphs that now felt like relics from another lifetime. When I finally found him, he was nowhere to be seen. A note was tucked under his usual reading glasses, crisp and impersonal, informing me of his departure. I picked it up, my hands trembling slightly, and reread the few lines. There was no warmth, no acknowledgment of the years we had spent building something together. A tightness constricted my throat, making swallowing difficult. I retreated to my desk, the paper feeling flimsy in my grip, and stared blankly at the monitor, the screen reflecting only a hollowed-out version of my own expression.

[story 3]

The morning started with a strange, leaden sluggishness. I made my usual cup of tea, but the warmth did little to penetrate the chill that seemed to have settled deep within my bones. I kept glancing toward the hallway, half-expecting to see him materialize, to offer some casual observation or shared joke. Nothing. The office felt cavernous, the usual hum of activity replaced by an unnerving quiet. I paced the length of the room twice, my footsteps echoing too loudly on the polished floor. When I finally checked the internal memo board, the space reserved for his weekly updates was conspicuously blank. A cold knot tightened in my stomach; it wasn't just the loss of a colleague, but the sudden absence of the anchor that had kept my own ambitions tethered. I leaned against the filing cabinet, closing my eyes, wishing the morning would simply dissolve.
